title: The 2,000-Year-Old Mental Jiu-Jitsu Move
hook: Patanjali's fix for a mind full of dark thoughts is NOT 'suppress them.' It's a jiu-jitsu move most people get wrong.

script.visual: Person typing a passive-aggressive reply to a work Slack. Finger hovers over Send. Hard cut to face.
script.voiceover: You're about to press send on something you'll regret. Patanjali has a move for this.

script.visual: Sanskrit appears: वितर्कबाधने प्रतिपक्षभावनम्. Two circles on screen — one labeled 'the dark thought,' the other 'its opposite.'
script.voiceover: Sutra 2.33. Vitarka-badhane pratipaksha-bhavanam. When a destructive thought grips you — pratipaksha-bhavana — cultivate its opposite. Deliberately. With detail. Not as denial. As a counter-move.

script.visual: Quick cuts illustrating the substitution: (1) angry thought about boss → imagining boss's 3 AM, (2) jealous thought about friend's job → recalling their 2-year grind, (3) contempt for a stranger → noticing their tired eyes.
script.voiceover: Here is how most people get this wrong. They think it means 'think positive thoughts.' It is not that. Patanjali is doing something sharper. You are angry at your boss. Do not pretend you love her. Instead: imagine her at 3 AM worrying about the same layoffs. You are jealous of your friend's promotion. Do not fake being happy for them. Instead: remember the two years they grinded in silence while you were watching Netflix. The opposite thought is not a lie. It is a wider truth.
script.text_overlay: It's not denial. It's a WIDER truth.

script.visual: Person sets the phone down. Deletes the draft. Writes something else.
script.voiceover: Your first thought is a snapshot. Narrow. Angled. The opposite thought forces you to widen the lens until the snapshot is embarrassing to send. That is the jiu-jitsu. You do not fight the angry thought. You use its own momentum to flip it into something truer. Then the urge to press send — just evaporates.
script.text_overlay: Widen the lens. The urge dissolves.

script.voiceover: It is not 'be nice.' It is 'see more.'
What's one dark thought from today you can flip by widening the lens? Drop it.

cta: Pick one angry, jealous, or contemptuous thought from today. Don't deny it. Widen it. Share what you see.
caption:
You are about to press Send on something you will regret. Patanjali has a move.

Sutra 2.33: वितर्कबाधने प्रतिपक्षभावनम्
vitarka-badhane pratipaksha-bhavanam

"When destructive thoughts obstruct you, cultivate the opposite."

Most people mis-read this as 'think positive thoughts.' That is not what Patanjali is saying.

He is teaching mental jiu-jitsu.

A dark thought is a snapshot. Narrow. Angled. One frame. You are angry at your boss — in that frame, she is only the person who dismissed your idea. You are jealous of your friend — in that frame, they are only the person whose promotion went public.

Pratipaksha-bhavana is not the lie 'I love my boss.' It is the wider truth: your boss is also the person who is probably losing sleep over the same layoffs. Your friend is also the person who worked two years in silence while you were on Netflix.

The opposite thought is not prettier. It is wider.

And here is the magic — you cannot hold a snapshot and a panorama at the same time. When the wider truth arrives, the angry snapshot quietly loses its grip. The urge to press Send evaporates.

It is not 'be nice.' It is 'see more.'

#yogasutras #patanjali #pratipakshabhavana #cognitivebehavioraltherapy #anger #jealousy #mentalhealth #yogaphilosophy #mindfulness #ancientwisdom #consciousness #psychology #emotionalregulation #yogadarshan #wisdom
music_suggestion: Tense, taut string tremolo for the 'about to press send' opening. Breaks into a clean, widening ambient pad at the word 'pratipaksha.' Lands on a single warm chord at the ending.
